turns.content: I'm trying to add some validation logic to the vec from a C library but I can't figure out how to make it look like my own type. I thought if I just declare a newtype struct and use DerefMut, that would automatically give me all the methods I need without writing extra code.

That's a common misunderstanding about how Rust handles foreign types. While you can create a newtype wrapper around an external vector, simply declaring it isn't enough to automatically delegate methods unless you explicitly implement the trait for your own type. The key here is that Rust doesn't magically copy over methods from a DerefMut target to the wrapper itself; you must manually write those method implementations on your wrapper struct.
So I still have to write all the functions myself? That seems like extra work compared to what I expected. Is there a way Rust can infer these?

Rust cannot infer method implementations for you in this scenario because it treats your wrapper as a completely new type by default, separate from the underlying foreign vector. Think of it like putting a box around a car; just having the box doesn't make the engine start or accelerate on its own—you still have to program how that box interacts with the car. You need to explicitly write functions like push_back and clear on your wrapper struct.
Okay, so I understand that now. But what about traits? Can't I just derive Serialize or Clone for my newtype automatically?

Traits like Serialize and Clone are different from methods; they are contracts that your type must fulfill to work with crates like serde_json. While you can often derive these traits if the underlying foreign vector supports them, sometimes you need to implement them manually depending on how the external library exposes its data. The distinction is important because methods are behaviors you define, whereas traits are capabilities you must prove.
I see. So if I want my wrapper to act like a standard vector but with extra safety checks, I should implement the trait and then write the methods?

Exactly. You would likely implement the trait first to ensure compatibility, then write your custom methods that delegate to the underlying foreign vector while adding your safety logic. This pattern lets you keep the interface familiar while layering on specific business rules without needing to modify the original C library code.
That makes sense. One more thing though, what if I want to avoid implementing all these methods and just reuse some standard ones?

You can leverage the Deref trait to get basic operations like indexing or length without writing them out manually, but you still need to implement anything that requires your custom logic. The Deref mechanism handles simple delegation automatically, so for most common vector operations, your wrapper will behave identically to the original type once you set up the deref correctly.
Thanks! I think I'm ready to start writing the wrapper now. Just want to confirm that this approach is better than using a generic adapter pattern?

For performance-critical code where you need tight integration with a specific foreign type, wrapping it directly with a newtype and manual method implementation often provides clearer ownership and avoids the overhead of generic adapters. It keeps the scope limited to just that one foreign type while giving you full control over how it behaves in your application.
